perturbation.original_text: The Applicant 's obligations her eunder shall survive until all information in the Confidential Information hereunder becomes publicly known.
perturbation.changed_text: The Applicant 's obligations hereunder shall survive for a period of 1 year from the date of disclosure, regardless of whether the information becomes publicly known.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligations to only 1 year contradicts standard practices and legal expectations for protecting sensitive information, especially considering the technical nature of the disclosed data. This fixed term may be insufficient to safeguard the Agency's interests, potentially violating legal requirements for data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits confidentiality to 1 year, which might be insufficient to protect trade secrets. The Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year limit could be deemed unreasonably short and fail to provide adequate protection, thus conflicting with the intent and potentially the requirements of trade secret laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the confidentiality obligation to one year, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, implying an indefinite protection period as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret. The one-year limit undermines this protection, creating a direct contradiction because it could allow for the legal disclosure of sensitive information after only a year, regardless of its continued proprietary value. Snippet 2 is a dead link.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute between the Agency and the Applicant in relation to the interpretation, application or validity of this Declaration which c annot be settled amicably shall be brought before the courts of Ljubljana.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute between the Agency and the Applicant in relation to the interpretation, application or validity of this Declaration which cannot be settled amicably shall be resolved through binding arbitration in a location chosen solely by the Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing binding arbitration chosen solely by the Agency removes the Applicant's right to seek legal recourse through established court systems in a fair and impartial venue. This can be seen as violating principles of fair dispute resolution and potentially contradicting laws related to contractual fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stipulating that the arbitration location is chosen solely by the Agency, the modified text potentially violates the principle of impartiality required in arbitration proceedings under the Federal Arbitration Act. Section 10 allows for vacating an arbitration award where there is evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators. This suggests that arbitration must be fair, which is questionable if one party has complete control over the venue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by forcing binding arbitration in a location solely chosen by the Agency, which undermines the Applicant's right to a fair and impartial dispute resolution process. This contradicts the principle of impartiality required in arbitration proceedings. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 U.S. Code § 10, allows for vacating an arbitration award where there is evident partiality in the arbitrators. The Agency's sole control over the arbitration location raises concerns about partiality, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable. The original text allowed disputes to be brought before the courts of Ljubljana, providing a neutral venue, while the modified text removes this option and replaces it with a process controlled by one party.

perturbation.original_text: • to immediately notify in writing the Agency i n the event of any unauthorised use or disclosure of the Confidential I nformation.
perturbation.changed_text: • to notify the Agency of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information within 30 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period to 30 days after discovery significantly hinders the Agency's ability to respond quickly and mitigate potential damages from unauthorized disclosure. Immediate notification is crucial for effective response and recovery, and a 30-day delay contradicts the standard of prompt action required in data breach scenarios.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various State Data Breach Notification Laws (example: California)
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.29 requires notification to affected individuals 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach. Delaying notification for up to 30 days directly contradicts the requirement for expedient notification, potentially violating California's data breach notification law if California residents are affected. While the contract is governed by the law of Slovenia, the applicant could be based in any location and therefore subject to their local laws.

(a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1798.29, which requires notification of a data breach to affected individuals 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay.' By changing the notification period to within 30 days of discovery, the revised contract introduces a delay that directly conflicts with the law's mandate for prompt notification. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that an agency shall disclose any breach of security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach. The 30-day delay could hinder the ability to take timely action in response to a data breach.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information remain the property of the Agency and no license or other rights in the Confidential Inform ation are granted hereby, except as expressly provided above.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information will be jointly owned by the Agency and the Applicant, with each party having the right to use the information for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the confidential information is jointly owned and can be used by either party for any purpose directly contradicts the fundamental principle of non-disclosure agreements, which aims to protect the disclosing party's proprietary information and prevent unauthorized use. This change undermines the entire purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation, which includes unauthorized use or disclosure. Granting the Applicant joint ownership and the right to use the Confidential Information for any purpose directly contradicts the UTSA's protection against misappropriation, as it eliminates the element of unauthorized use.
perturbation.location: Paragraph related to property rights of confidential information

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of protecting confidential information, as it grants joint ownership and unrestricted usage rights to both parties. This nullifies the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which aims to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of proprietary information. The Law Explanation indicates this change eliminates the protection against misappropriation by eliminating unauthorized use. While the scraped law snippets provided are unhelpful, the combined explanations of the perturbation and the law, along with the clear contrast between the original and changed text, demonstrate a significant legal contradiction.
